title: Brown Windsor Soup
subtitle: A thick, savoury beef and root vegetable soup, once a Victorian staple
excerpt: Brown Windsor soup is a thick, deeply coloured beef soup thickened with flour and flavoured with root vegetables, herbs, and a splash of Worcestershire sauce. It has a reputation as bland institutional food but made well at home with good beef shin and a long simmer it is a properly savoury winter soup. The colour comes from the beef and the brown flour, not from food colouring.
sourceNotes: Brown Windsor soup was a fixture of Victorian and Edwardian British cooking, served in institutions, boarding houses, and hotels. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: brown soup with beef and root vegetables. The Windsor reference is to Windsor Castle, where the soup was reportedly served to royal guests in the nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Defrost overnight and reheat gently, adding a little stock if it has thickened too much.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Makes an excellent batch-cook soup.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 3 days ahead and store in the fridge. The flavour improves on the second day.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cooking flour in a dry pan or with fat until it turns light brown, losing some of its raw starch taste and developing a nutty, toasted flavour. In brown soups and gravies, browned flour provides both thickening and a warm, dark colour without using artificial colouring.

content.text: The colour and flavour of Brown Windsor soup come from two things: well-browned beef and

before adding stock. Pale, unbrowned beef and raw flour stirred into white stock produces the grey, institutional version of the soup that gave it its poor reputation. The browning step takes an extra five minutes and transforms the result.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a high heat. Brown the {{beef-shin}} beef pieces in batches until deeply coloured on all sides. Remove and set aside. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, and {{celery}} to the pan and cook over a medium heat for 8 minutes until beginning to colour.

Stir in {{plain-flour}} of flour and cook for 2 to 3 minutes, stirring, until the flour smells slightly nutty and turns a light tan colour. Return the beef to the pan. Gradually add {{stock-beef}} of hot beef stock, stirring to prevent lumps. Add {{bay-leaves}}, thyme, {{worcestershire-sauce}}, {{sea-salt-flakes}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Bring to the boil, reduce to a gentle simmer, and cook partially covered for 1 hour to 1 hour 15 minutes until the beef is very tender. Remove the bay leaves and thyme sprigs before serving.

rows.cause: Beef and vegetables not browned sufficiently, flour not cooked
rows.fix: The browning step is not optional. Colour on the beef and vegetables, and colour on the flour before adding the stock, are what produce the characteristic dark, savoury soup.

rows.cause: Insufficient simmering time for the collagen in the shin to break down
rows.fix: Continue simmering. Beef shin needs at least an hour and sometimes longer depending on the size of the pieces. The meat should be pulling apart easily.

content.text: Brown Windsor soup was once a standard of British institution catering, served in hospitals, boarding schools, and railway dining cars from the Victorian era through the mid-twentieth century. Its association with institutional food has lingered, but the original home-cooked version, made with good beef and properly browned, is a different dish: a robust winter soup with real depth of flavour.
